cases.title: Gasping After Collapse
cases.presentation: A 58-year-old man collapses at his desk. You confirm the scene is safe, tap his shoulders, and shout — he does not respond. As you scan for breathing and feel for a carotid pulse, you see him take a single slow, snoring gasp every several seconds. You feel no pulse in 10 seconds.
cases.question: What is the correct interpretation and action?
options: A: He is breathing, so place him in the recovery position.
B: The gasps are agonal; treat as cardiac arrest and begin high-quality CPR.
C: Give two rescue breaths and recheck the pulse for 30 seconds.
D: Wait to see if the gasping becomes regular breathing.
cases.explanation: Occasional slow, snoring gasps are agonal breathing — a hallmark of early cardiac arrest, not effective breathing. With no pulse in 10 seconds, this is cardiac arrest: begin compressions immediately.
cases.clinicalPearl: Agonal gasps are one of the most commonly misread signs of arrest; when in doubt, start CPR.

cases.title: Unsafe Scene
cases.presentation: You arrive to find a person unresponsive on a wet garage floor near a running generator, with a faint smell of exhaust and an extension cord lying in a puddle beside them.
cases.question: What should you do first?
options: A: Rush in and start compressions immediately.
B: Ensure the scene is safe first — address electrical and possible carbon monoxide hazards, and move the patient/secure the scene before care.
C: Apply an AED while standing in the puddle.
D: Ignore the hazards because the patient is critical.
cases.explanation: The scene contains electrical (cord in water) and possible carbon monoxide hazards. Making the scene safe — securing power, ensuring ventilation, and moving to a safe/dry area — must come before hands-on care to avoid a second casualty.
cases.clinicalPearl: A dead rescuer saves no one. Scene safety is not a delay in good care — it is the first step of it.

cases.title: Fainted, Not Arrested
cases.presentation: A young woman faints at a warm outdoor event. She is briefly unresponsive when you reach her, but you confirm the scene is safe, and on assessment she is breathing normally with a strong, regular carotid pulse. She begins to open her eyes.
cases.question: What is the most appropriate BLS action?
options: C: Monitor her, protect her airway (recovery position if needed), and reassess; call for help if she does not improve.
D: Apply an AED and analyze the rhythm.
cases.explanation: A patient breathing normally with a strong pulse is not in cardiac arrest. Fainting (syncope) is managed by monitoring, positioning to protect the airway, and reassessing. Compressions and AED are not indicated when a pulse and normal breathing are present.
cases.clinicalPearl: Not every collapse is an arrest — match the intervention to the assessment findings, not to the drama of the moment.
